prompt:
On the last Saturday before our move, I pulled my grandfather’s bicycle from the garage and expected to wheel it straight to the curb. The tires were flat, the chain was orange with rust, and one handlebar grip had split open like a dry seedpod. My father, already surrounded by cardboard boxes, said the bike had not been ridden in twenty years. “Nobody will want that thing,” he told me. Still, I set it upright in the driveway.
Mr. Alvarez, our next-door neighbor, paused during his morning walk and asked whether I needed a wrench. He returned with tools, oil, and the kind of patience that makes silence feel useful. As we worked, he pointed out details I had never noticed: the bell engraved with a tiny star, the polished line on the frame where my grandfather’s shoe had brushed it for years, the hand-stitched cover on the seat. “A machine tells you how it was used,” he said. “You just have to look.”
By noon the bike was not exactly beautiful, but it was unmistakably alive. The wheels turned freely, and the bell gave off a bright, stubborn ring. I rode to the end of the block and back, past the maple tree where my grandfather used to wait for me after school. When I coasted into the driveway, my father looked up from a box and smiled in surprise. We did not talk about taking the bicycle to the curb again.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The passage argues that old bicycles are usually easier to repair than people expect.
choices.B: Restoring the bicycle helps the narrator recognize it as a meaningful link to his grandfather.
choices.C: The narrator decides to fix the bicycle mainly to impress Mr. Alvarez.
choices.D: The father regrets packing boxes before cleaning the garage.

explanation: The passage begins with the bike seeming worthless and ends with it clearly worth keeping because it carries family memory. The repair changes what the bicycle means to the narrator.

According to the passage, Mr. Alvarez helps the narrator primarily by:

choices.A: offering to buy the bicycle immediately
choices.B: reminding the narrator to ask his father for help
choices.C: bringing tools and patiently showing the narrator what to notice
choices.D: insisting that the bicycle be donated to a museum

explanation: Mr. Alvarez returns “with tools, oil, and the kind of patience that makes silence feel useful” and points out meaningful details on the bike.

prompt:
In July, a city planning class invited residents to help measure heat across three neighborhoods. Each volunteer carried a small sensor at waist height and followed a map for one hour near sunset. The class expected downtown blocks with taller buildings to be warmest, because brick and concrete store heat during the day. That assumption was only partly right.
When the readings were plotted, the hottest spots were not always the busiest ones. A shaded commercial street stayed several degrees cooler than a nearby parking lot, even though both sat in the city center. On the residential side of the study area, narrow streets with mature trees sometimes matched park temperatures, while wide intersections without shade remained hot well into the evening. The resulting map looked less like a smooth gradient and more like a patchwork quilt of warm and cool pockets.
Residents used the findings quickly. One neighborhood association asked for more bus shelters after volunteers noted how much cooler covered stops felt than open sidewalks. A grocery owner requested permission to paint his dark roof with a reflective coating. The class emphasized that the project did not produce one grand answer about “the city”; instead, it showed how small design choices—trees, pavement, shade structures, and roof color—can change daily comfort block by block.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Residents preferred bus shelters to parking lots for reasons unrelated to temperature.
choices.B: The study showed that neighborhood heat can vary sharply from block to block.
choices.C: Downtown areas are always warmer than residential areas at sunset.
choices.D: The class concluded that roof color matters more than every other design choice.

explanation: The passage emphasizes warm and cool “pockets” and ends by stressing that small design choices affect comfort block by block.

prompt:
Visitors often approach a painting label before they approach the painting itself. Museums do this unintentionally. A neat rectangle of text, mounted at eye level, announces that meaning has already been organized and made portable. The painting beside it, by contrast, may seem slow and stubborn. You must stand there, decide where to look, and tolerate a few seconds of uncertainty.
I do not argue for removing labels. Dates, materials, and historical context can sharpen attention rather than dull it. But the most helpful labels behave less like verdicts and more like invitations. Instead of announcing what a viewer is supposed to feel, they offer one or two questions worth carrying into the act of looking. Notice how the artist directs your eye. Notice which figure is turned away. Notice what disappears when you step back.
I once watched two students spend five minutes with a landscape they had nearly passed. The label did not decode the painting for them; it simply mentioned that the artist had returned to the same river bend in different seasons. Suddenly the students began comparing the sky to the reeds, the reeds to the water, and the summer version they imagined to the autumn one before them. Good interpretation did not replace looking. It gave looking somewhere to begin.
Question:
Which choice best states the author’s main point?

choices.A: Paintings should usually be displayed without any labels at all.
choices.B: Museum labels are most useful when they encourage viewers to look more carefully.
choices.C: Students understand landscapes better than adults do.
choices.D: Historical facts matter more than personal observation when viewing art.

explanation: The author does not reject labels; instead, the passage argues that the best ones act as invitations that deepen looking.

explanation: The first paragraph says viewers must “stand there, decide where to look, and tolerate a few seconds of uncertainty.”

Visitors often approach a painting label before they approach the painting itself. Museums do this unintentionally. A neat rectangle of text, mounted at eye level, announces that meaning has already been organized and made portable. The painting beside it, by contrast, may seem slow and stubborn. You must stand there, decide where to look, and tolerate a few seconds of uncertainty.
I do not argue for removing labels. Dates, materials, and historical context can sharpen attention rather than dull it. But the most helpful labels behave less like verdicts and more like invitations. Instead of announcing what a viewer is supposed to feel, they offer one or two questions worth carrying into the act of looking. Notice how the artist directs your eye. Notice which figure is turned away. Notice what disappears when you step back.
I once watched two students spend five minutes with a landscape they had nearly passed. The label did not decode the painting for them; it simply mentioned that the artist had returned to the same river bend in different seasons. Suddenly the students began comparing the sky to the reeds, the reeds to the water, and the summer version they imagined to the autumn one before them. Good interpretation did not replace looking. It gave looking somewhere to begin.
Question:
The first paragraph primarily serves to:

choices.A: argue that labels should contain more dates and material details
choices.B: contrast the immediate readability of labels with the slower work of looking at art
choices.C: show that most museum visitors ignore paintings completely
choices.D: explain how labels are physically manufactured and installed

explanation: The author sets up the neat, easy text of the label against the more demanding experience of viewing the painting itself.

choices.D: advertisements

explanation: In context, “verdicts” contrasts with “invitations,” so it means fixed conclusions about what the viewer should think or feel.

prompt:
On certain warm nights, paddles moving through a tropical bay leave bright blue trails in the water. The light is not produced by the paddles themselves but by microscopic dinoflagellates, plankton that flash when the surrounding water is disturbed. The effect can seem magical, yet the mechanism is practical: the sudden burst may startle tiny predators or signal that a grazer is nearby.
Researchers studying these bays track more than the number of glowing cells. They also monitor salinity, water temperature, rainfall, and the amount of sediment stirred up by storms. A bay may contain dinoflagellates year-round and still appear dim if cloudy runoff blocks sunlight needed by the algae those plankton depend on. In other cases, heavy rain lowers salinity enough to reduce the population temporarily, even when tourist boats report perfect darkness as if the organisms had vanished entirely.
For that reason, scientists caution against judging a bay’s health from a single nighttime visit. A weak glow may reflect short-term weather rather than long-term damage. Even so, repeated disturbance from engines and shoreline construction can harm the conditions these organisms need. Many tour operators now use paddle craft instead of motorboats, both to protect the habitat and to give visitors a clearer chance to see the light spread in quiet arcs.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Dinoflagellates vanish permanently whenever tourist boats report darkness.
choices.B: Scientists can predict a bay’s brightness by studying only water temperature.
choices.C: Bioluminescent bays glow because of microscopic organisms, and the brightness depends on changing conditions.
choices.D: Paddle craft create brighter light than motorboats because their paddles contain reflective materials.

explanation: The passage explains both the source of the light and the environmental factors that can alter how bright it appears.

choices.D: only when large predators enter the bay

explanation: The first paragraph explicitly says dinoflagellates “flash when the surrounding water is disturbed.”
